Who is your target audience? Now I don't know what product or service you're providing in what space. I can tell you, for example, we have an herbal supplement that we sell and it is primarily gage towards people who are 40, 45 plus. So that's the demographic. Our customers are about split on male and female, so that's not going to influence the advertising that I'm going to focus on. But that's my target audience and I know who that is. Some people create, as you're going through this exercise, identifying who are your customers. Some people will actually make caricatures. They'll go and they'll find a picture of somebody who fits that demographic. So they'll find some lady who's 55 plus and they'll print a picture of her and put her on the wall and maybe they'll name her Debbie. And they'll do the same thing with the gentleman. They'll have a man who fits her demographic, they'll put her on the wall and they'll name him Joe. And so every time they do something, when they're creating a product, they're looking at advertising options, they look at that picture and they say, okay, who is Joe? What activities is Joe involved in? Is he into hunting? Is he into fishing? Is he into sports? Is he into golf specifically? Debbie, is she into cooking? Is she into sports? Is she into fishing and hunting maybe? But where are these individuals congregating online? Russell talks a lot about focusing on marketing where people congregate. What are they buying? What are these customers buying? What news sites are they looking at? This is very important. What news sites are they looking at? People spend a lot of time on news sites. There's a lot of different stories they can look at. And we have found some of our most effective advertising is on news sites. Now some of these aren't your typical ABCs, NBC, Fox, those kind of things. Some of these are more, they're targeted to a more specific audience. So we've had a lot of success on more, with conservative news sites. All right? Don't tell me why. I didn't quite figure it out. What news sites are they looking at? Those are all important features. Okay. Now, it was important when you find a winner, meaning when you find a list that you've tested and you work on and it works, keep going. Don't stop with it. A good way to measure it is once you've mailed once, depending on the size of the mailing, if it's positive, meaning you've made some money, then you want to do two times that amount the next time. You don't want to jump to four or five times or 10 times the list size. You want to scale it smaller and just kind of go bigger and bigger and bigger. Typically those lists, you can, if there's a big list, you can ask if you can do half the list or even a quarter list or you can, for example, that world in that daily, we've got to deal with them where I think it's over about eight emails or mailings hits the whole list 100%. It's a press part of it. So what happens is that we mailed to, just like I said, one eighth of the list the first time and then the next week, we do another eighth. We do another eighth. We do another eighth. And then every time, they keep track of who saw the first email and they make sure that the next people don't see the same email. So you're hitting the whole list that way. All right. And when you find lists that work well, new sites that work well or other websites that work well, book future mailings. If it's positive for you, you don't want to have something working really well and all of a sudden, if he sold out, a friend of mine I was talking to, he's a list broker and he said, some of these lists work really well and other people see the same thing to get good results and so they buy it up for the whole year. And so there's no inventory available with that list. So again, if you find something that works, if it works once, it's going to probably work again. But again, scale is smart. Don't just blow it up. And list brokers and people like that, it's their expertise. They'll help you with that. Okay. So next point, how much does it cost? Now most lists charge using a model, a CPM model. Now CPM stands for cost per thousand. All right. So what happens? These lists, they have these lists and these companies, they know how many subscribers they have, right? That's part of their business. They know that typically a couple times a year, at least for sure by the end of the year, they purge and kind of go through and delete email addresses that are not active and things aren't happening with. So they really want to try and get an accurate number. But let's just say for example, if I contacted WorldNet Daily, for example, and they said, okay, the list size is a hundred thousand and the price is going to be $10 CPM. How I can figure out what it's going to cost me is I take a hundred, so a hundred thousands. Again, this is cost per one thousand, so a hundred times $10 gives me the amount it's going to cost me. All right. That's an easy way, it's easy to calculate that, but that's how much it's going to cost me to mail to a hundred thousand subscribers. Now with these kind of mailings, they don't guarantee clicks. Some solo email vendors and some people out there will say, hey, I'll get you X amount of clicks. Okay. That's not how this works. This is this model I'm talking to you about. It doesn't work like that. But again, it just depends. Now some lists, well, you'll find those are just flat rates. They may say, okay, it's $1,600 to mail to our list, or it's $5,500 to mail to our list. When you're starting out, again, it's new. You don't want to start out huge. You want to kind of scale it up when you have a new offer or you want to see how it does. So advertising, the advertising, and there's definitely a risk involved and I kind of want to just put that out there. If you're small, you can go bigger. As you have more experience, you can do it more often. And as you scale that offer up, you can do that. But again, it's advertising and there's always those risks. So keep that in mind, but start small and then go bigger. Okay. It's important to keep a method of tracking that. Now, I'm going to show you. I'll talk about that in a second here. And I keep track of that usually by date. So January 27, 2014 is when this went out. Now this is what I want to show you here is we've got organized by clicks, sales. If there's pending sales coming, the cost, how much I actually am paying for that and the profit. Okay. Now again, that doesn't cover the cost of the offer, but that's exciting. You can see all these things in green or positive. One's in red here or negative. And we did some different testing on these things, different lists of course. But knowing your numbers, tracking this stuff is very, very important. You need to know if what you're doing is winning or if you're losing. You need to know that. Okay. Okay. Let's get back to this. Okay. Okay. So know if you're winning or losing is very, very important. Okay. So email, copy and links. Now publishers, what happens when you contact a broker or contact a list owner, they're going to ask you for some things. Usually they want to look at the offer before they even say they'll accept it. So these publishers, they usually want copy that they can just copy and paste. They don't want to have to do a lot of work with that. So you can help them with it. So what we do, let me show you, for example, we just got a text file here. And this is our text file. And I have just, I've got some different versions of this. But I've got ahead and just going, you know, it's an HTML email copy. I've got our link here. So again, when they send an HTML email, this shows up differently. Okay. It's colorful and it's nice and pretty. But what's important here is I've got my links in here. There's my link there. There's another link here. This is some extra additional tracking that we're using for our redirect software that we use. But that's the email copy that we use. And that's it. This is a short version we're testing. I've got my subject line here, weird nerf paint miracle. And that's that. Okay. So what happens, I save this and it gets a text file so it doesn't mess up here. And we email it over to the list publisher and they take that and then they run that. What happens is once I give that to them, they will go ahead and they'll send me a test email, which looks exactly what the subscribers are going to get. Now what I do when I get that test email, I go and I click those links and I make sure that those links are taken to the correct page. I use what we call sub ID tracking, meaning the link pushes through there, but also I have an additional tracking on there so I can see which version of the email went out, what date it went out, if I want to look to make sure that it's all correct and how I set that up. All right. The more, the finer you can know your numbers and know your results, the better. Especially when you're testing, there's a lot of publishers will offer what they call, what we call AB split testing. So when we first started this campaign, we started out with different subject headlines. Yeah, subject lines. And so we had our same body copy and everything was the same, but just different headlines. We want to know which ones were getting clicks, which ones did people want to click on more. And so that was a good way to gauge it. And then once we found kind of a winner there, we went ahead and we test different copy. And we tested, for example, longer copy versus shorter copy. And we found that some of the shorter copy, at least for the software, it worked better than a longer copy. So we've been kind of going just with that shorter copy. And then we're going to be adding on a new test for that. So we'll take that control, that shorter copy, and we tried it with something different. We had a different picture, for example, in that ad that went out. And we saw the new version with a new picture did better than the old one. So again, you want to keep improving testing. It allows you to improve on the campaign. Now we use a retrex. We use a tool, a WordPress tool. If you're a WordPress site, you can use, it's a Ninja tool that you can use. Very, very important. Okay, so the last part here is I want to talk about some different resources. Now, again, I've got some people that I've worked with in the past. These are brokers. Now, these brokers, this is what they do full time. They are there in sales. They have publishers, meaning people who own different websites and websites that they own themselves. They want to sell advertising. That's how they're making the bulk of their money. They get people to the website, now they want to sell advertising to others. So what's really cool is they want you to be successful. It doesn't help them if you come by and add one time and you don't ever come back and purchase anything again. That doesn't help them. So they want you to come back. They want you to have success. Obviously, they want to make some money in the meantime, but then like more money over the long haul, if you keep coming back and buying ads, then a one time shot that you don't do very well and you give up and don't come back and buy more ads from them.
